critic_raw:
## Analysis

The methodology is substantially well-constructed: temporal splitting, single test-set pass, bootstrap CIs, ablation design, and SHAP auditing are all appropriate choices. I will focus on genuine structural issues rather than surface concerns.

---

### Issue 1: Preprocessing Fit on Full Cohort Leaks Future Distribution into Training

**Claim:** "Scaling and encoding transformers are parameterized once on the complete extracted cohort and then applied identically to the train, validation, and test partitions."

**Why this is wrong:** This is a classic look-ahead bias. When median imputation values, feature scaling parameters, and category vocabularies are estimated from the full 3-year dataset — including the validation and test periods — information about future distributional properties is baked into the training pipeline. This is not a hypothetical concern: lab reference ranges drift, medication formularies change, ICD-10 coding practices evolve. An imputed creatinine median estimated partly from year 3 data is not available to a model trained only on years 1–2 in a production deployment. The effect is likely small for stable continuous variables but can be meaningful for categorical vocabularies (e.g., new procedure codes appearing in year 3 would be treated as "known" during training) and for any variable that drifted across the 3-year window.

The correct approach is to fit all preprocessing transformers exclusively on the training partition and apply them forward. The stated justification — "keeps feature scales consistent across calendar periods so that differences between temporal cohorts are not conflated with differences in preprocessing" — confuses deployment realism with measurement consistency. In production, the preprocessing pipeline IS fitted on historical data only.

**Evidence for/against:** Refit all transformers on the training partition only and compare AUROC. If the leaked preprocessing inflated apparent performance, validation AUROC will drop. The magnitude will be most visible for categorical features with high cardinality or for labs with secular drift.

---

### Issue 2: Test Set Used for Model Selection Undermines Confirmatory Claim

**Claim:** Section 7 describes a "single confirmatory pass" on the held-out test period, but then explicitly permits selecting between model configurations based on which shows "more stable behavior on the test period" before locking the specification.

**Why this is wrong:** This is model selection on the test set, regardless of how it is framed. The design says: "If top validation config and a nearby alternative diverge on this out-of-period check, the configuration with more stable behavior on the test period will be retained." The test set is now a selection criterion. Any subsequent reporting of primary metrics from that same test set is no longer a held-out evaluation — it is in-sample to the selection procedure. The word "confirmatory" is doing false work here. The AUROC confidence intervals computed by bootstrap on this test set will be anti-conservative because they do not account for the selection variance.

This is the most structurally important flaw: it undermines the validity of the primary reported metric.

**Evidence for/against:** The design could be salvaged by either (a) using the test set strictly for reporting only, selecting exclusively on validation performance, or (b) pre-registering the tiebreaking rule with a specific threshold for what counts as a "tie" (e.g., <0.002 AUROC difference on validation) and committing to it before the test set is examined. Without one of these, reported test AUROC is an optimistically biased estimate.

---

### Issue 3: Model Complexity Confound Is Incompletely Controlled

**Claim:** The baseline (logistic regression) is compared against the hypothesis model (gradient boosted trees) to show the richer feature set, not the model class, drives improvement.

**Why this is wrong:** The comparison conflates two independent variables — feature set and model class — in the primary comparison. A GBT can model non-linear interactions and handle missing data natively in ways logistic regression cannot, so observed AUROC differences will include an unknown contribution from model complexity even when controlling for features. The ablation described (baseline + embeddings, no sequences) helps isolate feature contributions but still uses GBT throughout, so it doesn't address this confound.

The missing comparison is: GBT trained on baseline features only. Without this cell, the 5pp improvement threshold could be met entirely because GBT outperforms LR on the same features, not because sequential/note features add signal. This is a standard two-cell vs. four-cell design problem.

**Evidence for/against:** Add a GBT-on-baseline condition. If GBT-baseline ≈ LR-baseline in AUROC, the confound is negligible. If GBT-baseline > LR-baseline by ≥2-3pp, the reported improvement is partially attributable to model class, and the success criterion needs to be re-evaluated against the right counterfactual.

---

### Issue 4: Clinical BERT Embedding Strategy May Encode Leakage Through Note Content

**Claim:** Discharge summaries and final 24-hour notes are embedded and used as predictors; all predictor variables are constructed from information available at or before discharge.

**Why this is wrong:** The temporal anchoring claim is correct at the note level (discharge summaries are written at discharge), but the content of discharge summaries is not independent of the readmission outcome. Clinicians writing discharge summaries sometimes document planned or anticipated readmissions, social determinants suggesting high readmission risk, and explicit concerns about the patient's trajectory. More subtly, the very act of writing a longer or more detailed discharge summary is correlated with severity, which is itself correlated with readmission. This is not leakage in the strict causal sense, but it does mean the embeddings may capture clinician-perceived readmission risk that would not be available in a note-free baseline — i.e., the "signal" may be the clinician's own prediction, not independent predictive content.

Further, if the discharge summary is sometimes updated after the patient is re-admitted (which occurs in some EHR workflows), there is genuine temporal leakage. This should be verified.

**Evidence for/against:** (1) Audit whether any discharge summary timestamps fall after the readmission event in the linked records. (2) Run the ablation comparing model with vs. without discharge summaries and inspect SHAP values for whether embedding dimensions correlate with note-length or with explicit risk-language frequency. (3) If the model is dominated by discharge summary embeddings rather than the sequential lab/med features, this warrants deeper investigation into what the embeddings are capturing.

---

### Summary

Issue 2 (test set used for selection) is a genuine validity-threatening flaw that affects the primary reported metric. Issue 1 (preprocessing leakage) is a real but likely modest bias. Issue 3 (model complexity confound) means the success criterion is tested against the wrong counterfactual. Issue 4 is conditional on EHR workflow details but is worth auditing. Issues 1 and 3 share a root cause: the design conflates multiple independent variables without the necessary control cells.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers fit on the full cohort (including validation and test) introduce look-ahead bias into the training pipeline.
The test set is used as a tiebreaker for model selection before being used for confirmatory evaluation, undermining the held-out guarantee and making bootstrap CIs anti-conservative.

The primary comparison confounds feature set with model class (logistic regression vs. GBT); a GBT-on-baseline condition is missing, so observed improvements cannot be attributed solely to richer features.

Clinical BERT embeddings of discharge summaries may encode clinician-perceived readmission risk or be subject to post-hoc note updates, introducing subtle leakage that the temporal anchoring check does not fully prevent.

Preprocessing transformers (imputation medians, scaling parameters, category vocabularies) are fit on the full cohort before the temporal split, introducing leakage from validation and test periods into training-set feature construction.

The test set is explicitly used as a tiebreaker in hyperparameter selection (Section 7), which invalidates it as a held-out evaluation set and introduces an unknown positive bias in the reported AUROC.

If the clinical BERT model is fine-tuned or its embedding postprocessing (normalization, dimension selection) is fit on the full cohort, note embeddings carry leakage from the test period into the feature matrix.

The primary comparison conflates feature richness with model class (logistic regression vs. GBT); a GBT trained on baseline features only is missing, so the 5-point AUROC gain cannot be attributed to the new feature modalities rather than the more expressive model family.

AUROC as the sole primary success criterion at a 5% base rate may be met by improvements concentrated in the clinically irrelevant low-risk region; a minimum sensitivity at a specified operating threshold is absent from the success criterion.
Preprocessing fitted on the full cohort before splitting leaks test-period distributional information into the training pipeline.
The test set is explicitly used for tie-breaking model selection, invalidating its use as an unbiased evaluator and making the reported confidence intervals anti-conservative.

The hypothesis model (GBM) and baseline model (logistic regression) differ in both feature set and model class simultaneously, making it impossible to attribute the AUROC gap to feature richness alone.

Clinical BERT embedding statistics confound semantic clinical content with documentation intensity (note length, service complexity), and the proposed note-length stratification does not fully remove this confound.
The baseline model may be under-engineered relative to published readmission prediction benchmarks, artificially widening the apparent improvement gap.

assessor_results.critic_raw:
The methodology is reasonably well-constructed for a single-site EHR prediction study. The temporal split is correct, the label definition is explicit, and the confound controls are more thorough than average. However, there are several genuine methodological issues worth naming.

**Issue 1: Preprocessing parameterized on the full cohort before the temporal split**

The design states that 'scaling and encoding transformers are parameterized once on the complete extracted cohort and then applied identically to the train, validation, and test partitions.' This is a subtle but real form of data leakage. Median imputation values, continuous variable scaling parameters (mean, standard deviation, or min/max), and categorical vocabulary sets are all computed using future data (the validation and test periods) and then applied to training. In a temporally stable dataset this usually produces small numerical artifacts, but the stated justification—preventing confounding from calendar-period drift—actually reverses the correct reasoning: if there is meaningful drift in lab value distributions, medication patterns, or category vocabularies across years (as commonly occurs due to EHR upgrades, formulary changes, coding practice shifts), then using the full-cohort statistics actively obscures that drift rather than controlling for it. The correct procedure is to fit preprocessing only on the training set and apply the frozen transformers to validation and test. The current design will produce overestimated cross-period stability.

Evidence one way or the other: Compare imputation medians and scaling parameters computed on training-only vs. full-cohort. If they differ materially for clinically important variables (e.g., creatinine, BMP panels), this is a real problem. Also check whether test-set AUROC changes when preprocessing is refitted on training only.

**Issue 2: Model selection involves the test set before final evaluation**

Section 7 explicitly describes using the test set as a tiebreaker for hyperparameter selection: 'If the top validation configuration and a nearby alternative are effectively tied on validation performance but diverge on this out-of-period check, the configuration with the more stable behavior on the test period will be retained.' This is a direct violation of the held-out evaluation principle. Once the test set influences any part of the model selection decision—even a secondary tiebreak—it is no longer a held-out test set. The final AUROC reported on that same set will be optimistically biased for the chosen configuration. The bias may be small in practice if the configurations are genuinely close, but the correct response to a validation-set tie is to either use an additional held-out period, select based on parsimony (fewer parameters), or report both configurations. As described, the reported test AUROC has an unknown positive bias whose magnitude cannot be recovered post hoc.

Evidence one way or the other: If both tied configurations are evaluated on the test set and the reported number is taken from the one that performed better there, this inflates AUROC by construction. The magnitude can be estimated by comparing the two test-set AUROCs; if they differ by more than the confidence interval, the selection effect is non-trivial.

**Issue 3: BERT embedding leakage through global fine-tuning or vocabulary mismatch**

The design uses a 'pretrained clinical BERT model' but does not specify whether any fine-tuning or adapter training occurs on the study cohort. If the BERT model is fine-tuned (or even if its tokenizer vocabulary was adapted) on notes from the full cohort prior to the temporal split, then information from the validation and test periods is embedded in the note representations used as features. This is a standard leakage vector in NLP-augmented clinical prediction that is easy to miss because it occurs at the embedding layer rather than the feature layer. Even without fine-tuning, if mean/max embedding statistics are summarized across the full cohort to select which embedding dimensions to include or to normalize embeddings, the test set has influenced feature construction.

Evidence one way or the other: Verify that the BERT model weights were never updated on study-cohort text, and that embedding postprocessing (normalization, dimensionality reduction) was fit only on training-set notes. If either is true, a leakage-free replication would require re-running embeddings with a split-aware pipeline.

**Issue 4: Comparison conflates feature richness with model class — the ablation is incomplete**

The primary comparison is logistic regression on baseline features vs. gradient boosted trees on all features. This conflates two independent factors: (a) the additional feature types and (b) the more expressive model family. The design acknowledges this partially by noting the baseline ensures 'improvement is attributable to the richer feature set, not model complexity,' but a logistic regression on all features (or a gradient boosted model on baseline features only) is absent. The ablation in Section 8 isolates 'baseline + embeddings only' but still uses the more complex model. Without a GBT trained on baseline features only, the claimed 5-point AUROC improvement cannot be cleanly attributed to the feature modalities rather than the nonlinear interactions available to the tree ensemble. This is especially important if the baseline features include utilization counts and Charlson score, which are known to have nonlinear relationships with readmission risk.

Evidence one way or the other: Add a GBT trained on baseline features only as a second baseline. If this closes most of the gap with the hypothesis model, the feature modalities are not the driver.

**Issue 5: 7-day readmission base rate and AUROC as primary metric under class imbalance**

The design acknowledges the typical 5–15% base rate and argues AUROC handles class imbalance well. AUROC is indeed threshold-independent, but at a 5% base rate, large portions of the ROC curve are dominated by true-negative behavior, and small differences in AUROC correspond to clinically meaningful differences in sensitivity at realistic operating points. A 5-point AUROC improvement at a 5% base rate may translate to very different Net Benefit or sensitivity-at-fixed-FPR depending on where along the curve the improvement occurs. The design lists Net Benefit as a secondary metric, which partially addresses this, but the primary success criterion of ≥5 AUROC points should be complemented by a minimum sensitivity at a clinically specified threshold, since a model that gains 5 AUROC points entirely in the low-risk region is not useful for screening high-risk discharges.

Evidence one way or the other: Report where along the ROC curve the hypothesis model improves over the baseline (i.e., the difference curve). If the gain is concentrated at specificity > 0.95 (very low-risk patients), it is unlikely to generate clinical value at the intervention threshold.

**Summary:**
Issues 1 and 2 are genuine methodological flaws that affect the validity of the reported AUROC. Issue 1 (full-cohort preprocessing) introduces leakage by construction. Issue 2 (test-set tiebreaking) invalidates the held-out evaluation claim. Issues 3 and 4 are contingent on implementation details but represent known failure modes in this class of study. Issue 5 is a metric interpretation concern rather than a design flaw but is worth naming given the stated clinical use case.

issues_raised: Preprocessing fitted on the full cohort before splitting leaks test-period distributional information into the training pipeline.
The test set is explicitly used for tie-breaking model selection, invalidating its use as an unbiased evaluator and making the reported confidence intervals anti-conservative.

The hypothesis model (GBM) and baseline model (logistic regression) differ in both feature set and model class simultaneously, making it impossible to attribute the AUROC gap to feature richness alone.

Clinical BERT embedding statistics confound semantic clinical content with documentation intensity (note length, service complexity), and the proposed note-length stratification does not fully remove this confound.
The baseline model may be under-engineered relative to published readmission prediction benchmarks, artificially widening the apparent improvement gap.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
This is a well-structured methodology with genuine thought given to leakage, temporal splitting, and confound control. I will focus on the issues that could undermine validity rather than polish concerns.

**Issue 1: Preprocessing fitted on the full cohort before temporal splitting — a data leakage mechanism**

The design states: 'Scaling and encoding transformers are parameterized once on the complete extracted cohort and then applied identically to the train, validation, and test partitions.' The stated justification is that this prevents confounding preprocessing drift with temporal drift. But this is a subtle but real form of data leakage. Median imputation, scaling parameters (mean/std or min/max), and category vocabularies computed on the full cohort incorporate statistical information from the validation and test periods. For a globally stable variable (e.g., creatinine), the leakage is trivial. For variables that shift over the deployment period — new ICD-10 codes introduced mid-dataset, medications that became common only in year 3, lab panels added or discontinued — the test-period distributions silently inform the preprocessing applied to the training set. The practical consequence is that the model's feature representation is tuned to a population it should not yet have seen, artificially stabilizing performance. 

Evidence for or against: Refit preprocessing only on the training partition and compare AUROC on the test set. If the gap is small, leakage here is negligible. If AUROC drops materially or calibration degrades, the leakage was load-bearing. Also, inspect whether any high-importance features are ones known to be introduced or retired during the data window.

**Issue 2: Test set used for model selection, not just evaluation**

Section 7 explicitly states: 'If the top validation configuration and a nearby alternative are effectively tied on validation performance but diverge on this out-of-period check, the configuration with the more stable behavior on the test period will be retained.' This is model selection using the test set. Once any selection decision — even a tie-breaking one — is conditioned on test set outcomes, the test AUROC is no longer an unbiased estimate of out-of-sample performance. The stated rationale (preferring temporal stability) is clinically sensible but methodologically invalid. The reported confidence intervals, which are bootstrap intervals on the test set, do not account for this selection step, so they are anti-conservative.

The magnitude of bias depends on how often the tie-breaking rule fires and how similar the candidate configurations are. But the bias is structural: even rare use of the test set for selection inflates reported performance relative to what a truly held-out set would show.

Evidence for or against: Establish a strict policy that no model selection decision touches the test set. If a secondary test cohort (e.g., from a later date range or a different facility) is available, use it to verify that the locked model generalizes. Alternatively, report the exact number of times the tie-breaking rule was applied and conduct a sensitivity analysis removing those configurations.

**Issue 3: Confounding between model complexity and feature set in the primary comparison**

The hypothesis model is a gradient boosted ensemble; the baseline is logistic regression. The design justifies this by saying 'any improvement is attributable to the richer feature set, not model complexity.' But this claim is not supported by the design. GBMs routinely outperform logistic regression on tabular clinical data even when the features are identical, due to their ability to capture non-linear interactions and feature-threshold relationships that LR cannot represent without manual engineering. The two models differ simultaneously in features AND in function class, making it impossible to attribute the observed AUROC gap to either cause alone.

The ablation described in Section 8 (baseline + embeddings only, no sequences) does not resolve this because it still uses GBM and still has a different feature set from the LR baseline.

What would constitute evidence one way or the other: Add a GBM trained on baseline features only. If that model matches or approaches the hypothesis GBM, the improvement is due to model class, not feature richness. If the LR hypothesis model (baseline + sequential + embeddings) outperforms both the LR baseline and the LR-with-rich-features model, the contribution of non-linearity can be disentangled. Without this cell, the headline claim is not supported.

**Issue 4: Clinical BERT embeddings may capture documentation intensity, not clinical state**

The note embedding approach uses mean/max/percentile statistics from discharge summaries and final 24-hour notes. Long, complex, uncertainty-laden notes are likely to have different embedding statistics than short, routine notes — not because the underlying clinical state is different, but because documentation practice differs by service, attending, patient communication level, and social complexity. These same factors are correlated with readmission independently of clinical trajectory. The 'confound controls' section acknowledges note length as a concern and proposes stratification by note length, but stratification at a coarse level does not remove the confound — it merely shows whether it is the dominant driver.

The embedding statistics (mean/max/percentile) are also sensitive to note length directly, because averaging over more tokens changes the aggregate statistics. If a model learns 'high variance in embedding percentiles = uncertain/complex note = high readmission risk,' it is partially recovering a documentation intensity signal rather than a clinical content signal.

Evidence for or against: Train a model using only note length and keyword counts (no embeddings) and compare its AUROC to the full hypothesis model. A small gap would imply the deep embedding features are mostly acting as proxies for documentation intensity rather than semantic content. Additionally, within a stratum of matched note length and service line, if embedding features retain predictive value, the semantic signal is real.

**Issue 5: Single-system data limits generalizability claims, but more importantly, the baseline may be artificially weak**

The baseline uses ICD-10 presence/absence rather than coded comorbidity burden in a richer form, and uses only 6- and 30-day prior utilization windows. Many published readmission models (LACE+, modified HOSPITAL score, Epic's proprietary model) achieve AUROC of 0.70–0.78 on similar populations using only structured data. If the baseline here falls below that range, it may be under-engineered, making the 5-point gap easier to achieve. Conversely, if the gap disappears when compared to a well-tuned baseline, the hypothesis does not hold.

Evidence for or against: Compare the baseline AUROC against published benchmarks for the same outcome (7-day readmission, acute inpatient) on similar populations. If the baseline underperforms literature benchmarks by more than a few points, document the reason (population differences, narrower label, etc.). Report the baseline AUROC explicitly before claiming a 5-point improvement is meaningful.

**Summary of root cause groupings:**
- Leakage (Issues 1 and 2): Both the preprocessing-on-full-cohort and the test-set-used-for-selection issues stem from insufficient separation between the data used to set model parameters (broadly defined) and the data used to estimate generalization.
- Attribution confounding (Issues 3 and 4): The inability to separate feature contribution from model class, and the inability to separate semantic note content from documentation intensity, both undermine the causal claim that richer features explain performance gains.
- Baseline calibration (Issue 5): An under-engineered baseline inflates the apparent improvement size.

Of these, Issues 2 and 3 are the most structurally damaging. Issue 2 directly invalidates the confidence intervals on the primary metric. Issue 3 means the headline claim — that sequential and note-derived features improve prediction — cannot be confirmed from the described design without adding the missing experimental cell.
